data.title: American States Water's Quality Is Intact, Its Valuation Isn't
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that American States Water (NYSE:AWR) once sat on a throne of century-old prudence and relentless dividend hikes, but inflation, climate costs, clogged supply lines and a crankier regulator have ripped away the safety net, forcing management to thread every capital dollar through a needle even as the stock’s price tag still pretends predictability is a given.

American States Water Company Under Pressure

American States Water has long been the classic “set-and-forget” holding, its 69-year streak of dividend hikes embodying dependability. Yet stubborn wage inflation, tangled supply chains, elevated borrowing costs and a surge of pricey environmental mandates now collide with regulators who hesitate to approve full cost pass-throughs, forcing even this stalwart utility to rethink how it spends and how quickly it can earn those dollars back.

The squeeze is already showing up in the credit sheets.

Last year, a handful of utilities took downgrades after regulators cut back or delayed rate hikes. Then came Clyde, Texas, where a water-system failure turned deferred maintenance into a full-blown liquidity mess in just one quarter. Because the Environmental Protection Agency pegs required water and wastewater investment at roughly $1.2 trillion over the next 20 years, utilities that fall behind risk becoming price takers just when contractors are charging a premium for scarce crews and materials. AWR, which operates amid California’s strict oversight, therefore finds its trademark regulatory finesse necessary but no longer sufficient; it has to prioritize projects with almost surgical precision, lest capital dollars get trapped in queues while interest accrues on undepreciated assets.

Stack climate on top and things get worse. Longer fire seasons now chew up vegetation around Bear Valley’s electric lines, hotter summers increase system load, and rising insurance premiums quietly eat margin. Looking farther out, the looming post-2026 reduction in Colorado River allotments could tighten supply for the Southwest systems Golden State Water relies on, nudging treatment costs higher just when every penny matters. Federal disaster relief, once a reliable backstop, may also become a slower or smaller trickle as the Federal Emergency Management Agency juggles competing claims; consequently, utilities have to build thicker balance-sheet buffers of their own.

Yet, almost paradoxically, Washington’s policy pendulum is swinging in a way that re-energizes the electric side of the ledger. Because the Trump administration’s permitting reforms shorten approval timelines and the push for energy resilience enjoys bipartisan applause, grid expansion projects are moving from slide decks to shovels. After roughly 2 generations of demand stagnation, U.S. electricity consumption is now forecast to accelerate faster than at any point in the past 50 years, driven largely by data centers that alone could need an incremental 50 gigawatts by 2030. You may have noticed: in the first quarter of 2025 the S&P Utility Index rose 4.9% and the median regulated utility returned 9%, signaling that markets are willing to fund growth so long as earnings visibility improves. Even AWR’s relatively small Bear Valley Electric Service, which earned $0.07 a share this past quarter versus $0.05 a year ago, stands to benefit from the density of demand in California’s high-tech corridors.

Management’s ability to harvest that policy tailwind showed up early in 2025. Earnings per share climbed to $0.70, beating consensus by $0.03, while revenue grew 9.44% to $148.01 million. Those numbers sprang directly from the California Public Utilities Commission’s long-awaited approval of new rate cases: Golden State Water booked $0.52 a share, up from $0.48, and Bear Valley Electric pocketed the previously mentioned $0.07. Capital deployment is ramping in tandem. For calendar 2025, AWR intends to invest between $170 million and $210 million into regulated assets, and the CPUC has green-lit Golden State Water to lay out $573.1 million over the 2025–2027 span with mechanisms in place for prompt revenue recovery once projects close. Bear Valley has authority to spend $75.6 million, of which $23.1 million is fresh work expected to add roughly $3 million of annual revenue when rolled into rates, so the company can grow its electric rate base without stretching leverage.

As a matter of fact, the base is already expanding briskly: Golden State Water’s adopted average rate base went from $752.2 million in 2018 to $1.36 billion in 2024, a compound annual growth rate of 10.3%. If management executes its current pipeline on schedule, that upward trajectory should persist through the end of the cycle, cushioning earnings against the sector-wide cost creep that shows no sign of abating. Meanwhile, the contracted-services arm, American States Utility Services, did its part, contributing $0.13 a share as it assumed operations at Naval Air Station Patuxent River and Joint Base Cape Cod and secured $56.5 million in new military upgrade awards. Because construction activity was only modestly slowed by weather, ASUS entered Q2 with a record backlog, providing yet another lane of earnings visibility.

Crucially, AWR can pursue these investments without compromising its fortress balance sheet. Operating cash flow in the first quarter held steady at $45.1 million; a $50 million private note placement was completed, the revolving credit facility expanded to $195 million and pushed out to 2029, and the company still flaunts high-grade ratings of A from Standard & Poor’s, A+ for Golden State Water and A2 at Moody’s. Shareholders also keep seeing their checks grow: the dividend advanced from $0.45 in 2005 to $1.79 in 2024, rising at an 8.8% compound pace over the past 5 years and comfortably above management’s 7% target.

For anyone who invested $10,000 back in 2004 and reinvested distributions, the stake is now worth more than $74,000, a 10.3% annualized lift that even beats the S&P 500’s 8.12% over the same stretch. To fund growth without over-dialing debt, the at-the-market equity program has raised $117 million toward a $200 million goal; paced issuance means dilution stays manageable while fresh capital arrives just in time for shovel-ready projects.

American States Water Company: Challenges, Growth, and Valuation

Yet even as management leans into those policy-driven growth avenues, the very act of accelerating capital spend brings a fresh reckoning with valuation. Because every new dollar deployed eventually has to translate into durable, per-share returns, the next logical question is whether the market is already paying for that upside, or, perhaps, paying too much.

For anyone who has long treated American States Water Company as a sleepy, bond-like holding, the latest batch of figures lands with the uneasy realization that the company’s once-placid fundamentals and its still-premium valuation appear to be drifting apart.

Management concedes that the extra shares sold through its at-the-market program reduced first-quarter earnings by $0.02 a share, and because another $83 million to $85 million of issuance is slated to run through 2026, it is difficult to dismiss the suspicion that each incremental raise will keep chipping away at reported EPS. While fresh equity does spare the balance sheet some leverage, an ordinary investor, glancing at a brokerage screen rather than an infrastructure financing model, naturally focuses on the near-term dilution, and the tension between funding growth and protecting shareholder value becomes immediately palpable.

Dilution is only the first brick through the window of a company that once sold itself as utility royalty. In the same quarter, the pension portfolio coughed up a five-cent hit to earnings, erasing last year’s gain. Then came the rain-soaked slowdown at ASUS, shaving $1.8 million off revenue and turning a federal-contracting side into another line-item headache. One by one, these “one-offs” are lining up like a chain of bar tabs on the income statement. Management swears those weather-stalled dollars will show up later, but the mere fact that timing glitches can rattle a supposed bunker-grade stock shows just how much the earnings fog has rolled in.

What used to be a cozy regulatory breeze has turned into a coin-flip forecast. After dumping its old revenue-decoupling setup for a Monterey-style Water Revenue Adjustment Mechanism in January 2025, AWR’s California unit no longer enjoys the cushion that once muffled swings in consumption and supply costs. To be sure, first-quarter results benefitted fortuitously from a cheaper mix, more pumped water and less expensive purchased supply, but management acknowledged that the mix could easily tilt the other way in the next quarter. What nags is the sense that the California Public Utilities Commission, having already denied AWR’s request to preserve the old decoupling approach, may leave the company exposed to volume risk just as conservation efforts and climate volatility make demand forecasting trickier. Even if AWR prevails on rehearing and re-instates some of the prior protections, the episode hints that regulators are willing to ratchet up earnings variability at precisely the moment investors crave stability.

Operating costs keep climbing and not just because everything costs more: even after stripping out supply, AWR shelled out another $2 million year-over-year, much of it for wildfire and vegetation work that regulators now let the company wedge into the rate base, fine for recovery, brutal for the baseline. With labor and materials still priced like artisanal gold, any new uptick can pinch margins, especially under a rate formula that no longer cushions earnings from volume swings. Stack that slow burn on top of share dilution and the tidy utility narrative starts to unravel. Over in the federal-contracting shop, the ballast has slipped its moorings: no 50-year military privatization deals on deck for 2025, and the shorter 15-year versions demand more red tape for skinnier risk-adjusted payoffs, turning what once offset California volatility into another source of timing and execution headaches.

Put all that smoke-alarm spending and contract slippage next to a share count that keeps inching higher, and you finally have to look up at the scoreboard: price. Shares equate to 24.03× trailing earnings; by contrast, management’s own modeling, or at least consensus, pegs fair value at 15×, rising only to 20.93× by 2027.

Put differently, the market price is roughly 60% above the calculated fair value of $48.39, a gap that implies a growth trajectory far more vibrant than the numbers suggest.

Looking forward, EPS is expected to inch ahead 3.68% in 2025, 5.73% in 2026, and top out at 7.19% in 2027 before moderating again.

Those percentages, respectable for a regulated utility, nevertheless struggle to justify a forward P/E of 23.58, fully 34% higher than the sector median. Likewise, the PEG ratio of 4.17 versus the peer group’s 2.50 means investors are effectively paying almost twice as much per unit of growth.

All of this feeds into an estimated annual shareholder return of -8.87% (per FAST Graphs data above), a figure that hinges more on the sober math of multiple compression. The risk is not that the dividend, targeted to grow 8% annually, will vanish overnight; rather, the worry is that payout growth outstrips earnings expansion, forcing a choice between balance-sheet strain and a slower dividend trajectory. Profitability metrics already hint at pressure: EBITDA growth of 4.52% sits about 41% below the five-year average and trails the sector’s 8.82%, while EBIT growth is down nearly 60% from trend.

Revenue still advances at 6.79%, yet that top-line strength fails to flow through, suggesting either rising input costs or tariff structures that do not fully capture inflation.

Cash-flow trends, on the surface, look sparkling: operating cash flow surged 85.85% year over year and could rise another 47.23%. But context matters: capital expenditure is growing more than 34%, roughly quadruple the sector norm, raising the possibility that the working-capital tailwinds inflating cash-flow growth are transient. If so, free cash flow could end up thinner than headline figures imply, exactly when investors need proof that the dividend can be funded without leaning on incremental share sales or debt.

Final Takeaway

I’d label AWR a Hold because the shares already trade well above fair value, making fresh upside hard to pencil out, yet the company’s balance-sheet strength, multi-decade dividend record, and newly approved California rate base still offer a cushion against steep downside. Put simply, the stock looks too expensive to buy but too well capitalized, thanks to investment-grade credit, ample liquidity, and visible cap-ex recovery mechanisms, to dump outright. I’d wait for either a valuation pullback or clearer margin erosion before moving to a stronger stance.

data.title: BancFirst Corporation: Shares Are Close To Justifying A Downgrade
data.description:
Back in early March of this year, one financial firm that I decided to dig into was BancFirst Corporation (NASDAQ:BANF). With a market capitalization of $4.10 billion, the bank is far from small. In fact, it's on the larger end of the spectrum insofar as banks that I regularly analyze are concerned. At the time, I was turned off by how expensive shares were, both in relation to earnings and book value. However, I found myself impressed by the continued growth of the company and by its overall asset quality. These factors caused me to adopt a more neutral assessment of the business. And at the end of the day, I ended up settling on a ‘hold’ rating for it.

Whenever I rate a company a ‘hold’, I am making the claim that shares are likely to perform more or less along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. In the time since that article, BancFirst Corporation has come pretty close to that. While the S&P 500 is up 5.4%, shares of this particular name are up 6.8%. Unfortunately, this does make the stock more expensive than it was previously. But shares, as expensive as they are, are probably justified, if only marginally so, by the aforementioned factors of growth and asset quality. Because of this, I'm not ready to downgrade the company yet. But if we do see shares continue to rise like this, a downgrade would not be too far off.

Still not banking on BancFirst Corporation

I think a good place to start with BancFirst Corporation would be the results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. This is the only new data that has come out since my previous article about the company. During that time, deposits for the firm came in at a whopping $12.13 billion. This represents a nice increase over the $11.72 billion the business reported at the end of last year. And it's up from the $10.70 billion the company had on its books in 2023.

Whenever I see continued growth on the deposit side of things, especially in an environment that has proven to be challenging for banks because high interest rates have caused a rise in competition for deposits, I always worry about exposure to brokered deposits. As I have said ad nauseam, brokered deposits are not inherently bad. But they are more expensive than traditional deposits, and they are not always reliable. The good news is that management asserts that the firm does not rely heavily on long-term borrowings, and it does not utilize brokered deposits. This certainly helps to maintain asset quality at elevated levels. This does not mean that there aren't any negative aspects to the deposit situation for the business. At the end of the most recent quarter, 34% of its deposits were uninsured. This does create an elevated risk when it comes to potential bank runs. And even though it's not astronomically high, it is above the 30% minimum threshold that I like to see in this space.

Deposit growth also brought with it a growth in loans. From the end of last year to the first quarter of this year, loans expanded from $7.93 billion to $7.99 billion. The greatest exposure that the company has to loans would be the $1.63 billion that it has in non-owner-occupied commercial real estate. This works out to approximately 20.1% of the total loan portfolio. A close second would be residential real estate that is first lien at $1.45 billion, or 17.9% of its loans. Commercial non-real estate loans come in third at $1.35 billion, or 16.7%.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve some attention also. The value of securities, for instance, totaled $1.17 billion in the latest quarter. That compares to the $1.21 billion reported the previous year. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents increased markedly from $3.32 billion to $3.97 billion. As I mentioned earlier, management asserted that the company does not rely terribly on debt. And sure enough, that is the gospel truth. In the most recent quarter, the company had $86.2 million of debt on its books. And that represented a small decline from the $89.1 million the business had at the end of last year.

Moving on to the income statement, we have seen continued improvements from the firm. Even though its net interest margin remained flat at 3.70% year over year, net interest income popped from $102.1 million to $114.4 million. Some of this can be attributed to the overall growth in the balance sheet of the company. However, the firm also benefited from a reduction in its provision for credit losses from $4 million to $1.6 million.

Non-interest income has historically been a big part of the company's revenue stream and is also responsible, in part, for its elevated asset quality. The firm had $48.9 million worth of non-interest income in the first quarter of 2025. That compared favorably to the $44.9 million reported the previous year. Growth in multiple areas made this possible. As an example, service charges on deposits grew from $16.4 million to $16.8 million. The firm also enjoyed a rise in insurance commissions from $9.5 million to $10.4 million. Trust revenue continues to grow as the trust side of the company expands, with revenue rising from $5.1 million to $5.5 million. But the big driver of this expansion was cash management revenue. Year over year, this grew significantly from $8.7 million to $10.1 million. This part of the company includes the firm's sweep accounts and other related activities. The funds in these accounts are taken and invested on a short-term basis for the benefit of the company and its account holders. Year over year, these assets grew from $4.6 billion to $5.5 billion.

Thanks to these improvements, net profits for the company also improved, climbing from $50.3 million to $56.1 million. This continues a trend of continuous growth on the bottom line, with net profits rising from $193.1 million in 2022 to $216.4 million in 2024. And in all likelihood, continued growth by the company will allow 2025 figures to come in even higher than 2024 figures were.

Unfortunately, shares of the business are anything but cheap. In the chart above, you can see its price to earnings multiple compared to the price to earnings multiple of five similar companies. Four of the five firms are cheaper than it is. Furthermore, the price to earnings multiple of 18.9 that it's trading at places it meaningfully above the 6 to 10 range that I like to see in this industry. In the chart below, you can see a similar phenomenon. The price to book and price to tangible book multiples are significantly higher than most other banks. And in fact, it is higher than any of the five companies I compared it to.

Normally, this would necessitate a ‘sell’ rating on my end. But between the growth the company has seen and its overall asset quality, I have been more flexible. Speaking of asset quality, in the first chart below, you can see the firm's return on assets. The 1.66% is meaningfully higher than the 1% minimum threshold that I like to see. It's also quite a bit above four of the five companies I compared the business to. In the subsequent chart, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 13.85% reading that we get here is meaningfully above the 10% minimum threshold that I like to see. On top of this, it places the company above three of the five competitors I stacked it up against.

One other thing that deserves to be touched on is that, in May of this year, management announced that they were purchasing another bank. This is none other than American Bank of Oklahoma. This is a rather small purchase for the company, with management not even revealing the terms of the purchase. American Bank of Oklahoma has about $385 million in total assets, including $280 million in loans. Deposits amounted to $320 million. This transaction is expected to close in the third quarter of this year and will at least add to the overall size of BancFirst Corporation.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I believe that while BancFirst Corporation is more expensive than I am typically comfortable with, the firm is not exactly a bad candidate. It's not cheap enough in order to justify a ‘buy’ rating in my book. But it is cheap enough, if only marginally so, to justify keeping as a ‘hold’ candidate.

data.title: Atrium Mortgage:A High Yield Canadian Real Estate Idea
data.description:
Introduction

As it has been six months since I last discussed Atrium Mortgage Investment (TSX:AI:CA) (OTCPK:AMIVF), an alternative lender in the Canadian mortgage space, I felt this is a good moment to have another look at this high-yield income idea.

Robust results with low loan loss provisions

In the first quarter of the current financial year, Atrium reported a total revenue of just under C$22M. Although that’s a decrease compared to the C$25.2M in Q1 2024, the operating expenses also decreased due to the lower loan loss provisions while on top of that, the net interest expenses also decreased by almost C$1.5M.

This ultimately resulted in a pretty flat net profit of just under C$12M, which represented approximately C$0.25 per share. Atrium currently pays a monthly distribution of C$0.0775 per month, which works out to C$0.2325 per quarter, which means the distribution is fully covered based on the earnings, including a quarterly loan loss provision of approximately C$2.2M.

At the end of the first quarter, the total equity value on the balance sheet was approximately C$520.5M. Divided over the current share count of 47.4M shares, this represents a book value of just under C$11 per share, which means the stock is currently trading just above its book value. A large portion of the portfolio consists of high-rise and mid-rise residential units (which make up almost 44% of the total loan book) and at the end of the first quarter the average remaining term was nine months while the loans had an average interest rate of 9.56%.

As shown above, the vast majority of the C$875M mortgage book consisted of loans that are classified as Stage 1 loans, while the C$19.5M of mortgages classified as Stage 3 were covered by a C$11.1M mortgage loss allowance. Atrium has been dealing with its weaker loans in the past, and on its Q1 conference call, Atrium’s management explained it expects to report a reversal of a loan loss provision based on a loan secured by three Toronto office buildings. The company’s management also mentioned its loan loss provision on the other Stage 3 loan is "adequate."

A new convertible debenture

The company recently also announced it's issuing C$30M in convertible debentures. These debentures will be issued at 100% of par value and offer a 6% interest rate for the next 7.25 years (the scheduled maturity date is on September 30, 2032) while the debt can be converted into common shares at a conversion price of C$13.65. This helps the corporation to diversify its financing sources and to add duration to its financing structure. Atrium will use the funds to repay debt under its operating credit facility. Even though the latter has a lower cost of debt (5.38% in Q1), it makes sense to reduce the amount drawn, and just tap back into the credit facility when Atrium sees investment opportunities.

I quite like the convertible debentures offered by Atrium, and I currently own some of the ones that were previously issued and are currently trading on the Toronto Stock Exchange. I did not participate in the current raise (as the book runners didn’t accept offshore investors) but I will try to buy some once the convertible debentures start trading on the exchange.

Investment thesis

I currently have no exposure to the common shares of Atrium Mortgage, but I own some of Atrium’s convertible debentures as I was able to buy those securities at a discount to par, which resulted in a very attractive yield to maturity. I’m also quite interested in the newly issued 6% convertible debentures, and I will try to establish a long position just below par when these securities start trading.

I acknowledge the current dividend yield is higher than the yield offered by the debentures, but I personally prefer debentures as the interest payments are taxed at a different rate in my jurisdiction, and in combination with the senior status in the capital structure, I’ll stick with the debentures for now.

That doesn’t mean the common shares couldn’t be interesting, at the current share price, the dividend yield is quite attractive, and I could be interested in obtaining a long position on a weak day in the markets.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Fed's Faced With Macroeconomic Complexity
data.description:
My recent article on the latest CPI inflation data was titled "May CPI Inflation: Start Of A Nail-Biting Macroeconomic Drama?". Even though the inflation figure had moved up just a tad, it could be the start of an upward trend. With this as the background, the latest Federal Open Markets Committee (FOMC) statement was even more significant than usual.

While the Fed didn't feed into the sense of drama at all, toning down the statement from the previous month, a look at its projections over time does raise a concerning question. Here, I discuss the statement first, followed by a look at the updated economic projections. The implications for monetary policy are discussed next, and finally, the best investments for right now are touched upon.

The Fed tones it down

The FOMC statement is broadly unchanged except in one way. The Fed is less tilted towards the concerning state of the real economy, though it also rolls back on inflation-related stresses as well. Here's how.

  • Remark #1: The statement mentions that "Uncertainty about the economic outlook has diminished but remains elevated." This compares with "Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further." This suggests that while concerns for the economy still persist, they are less so than earlier.
  • Remark #2: Next, in May, the Fed was of the view that "The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate and judges that the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen". It now restricts itself only to the first part of the sentence. It says, "The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate." This deletion also indicates a less pointed concern about either growth or inflation, keeping the perspective balanced.

This, of course, raises the question - why? What has happened since May that the Fed is far more even in its statement? The answer lies in the latest data trends.

CPI inflation might have inched up a bit, but it still came in lower than expected in May. On the real economy front, the unemployment rate remained unchanged for the third consecutive month. These suggest that the negative impact of tariffs hasn't shown up, at least not yet, and that can be the reason for small ease.

Projections for 2025 reflect tariff impact...

At the same time, it's hard to miss that the Fed's updated economic projections do, in fact, reflect the impact of trade policy. The reserve now sees reduced GDP growth and higher inflation this year, though the projections for the policy rate remain unchanged. Here's how it sees these pieces fitting together:

  • GDP growth impacted by net exports

What's the latest forecast: GDP growth has been cut to just 1.4% from the earlier 1.7%, while the unemployment rate is seen just 0.1 percentage points higher (see table below).

Why not more rate cuts: The Fed pointed to the "unusual swing" in net exports in the first quarter at the press conference following the FOMC meeting as complicating GDP measurement. At the same time, it points to robust private domestic final purchases.

Also, its unemployment rate projections have shifted up ever so slightly for the full year (see table below), suggesting that the real economy isn't quite as impacted as the GDP numbers would suggest.

  • PCE inflation expected to rise faster

What's the latest forecast: Inflation based on PCE is now expected to increase by 3%, 0.3 percentage points higher than the previous estimate of 2.7%. Core PCE inflation is also expected to be higher now, at 3.1% compared with the earlier forecast of 2.8%.

This is consistent with an increase in "Near-term measures of inflation expectations", as per the Fed. The phenomenon is explained in surveys of various economic agents as being driven by tariffs.

Why not raise rates: While these forecasts are indeed higher than the Fed's comfort levels, the reserve explains that "longer-term expectations remain consistent with our 2 percent inflation goal". In fact, by 2027, it forecasts inflation to tend towards these target levels.

....but the economy looks different in 2026

From 2026 onwards, though, macroeconomic trends paint a different picture as far as the trajectory of interest rates goes. Both headline and core PCE inflation are expected to drop substantially next year and tend toward the Fed's target level of 2% by 2027.

GDP growth is still expected to remain sub-trend at 1.6%, though. This can explain why the median projection for the Fed Funds rate declines to 3.6% from 3.9% in 2025. However, it still remains higher than the earlier projection of 3.4%, likely as other aspects of the real economy, namely the unemployment rate, aren't expected to show any drastic changes. A similar trend is expected for 2027.

Essentially, then, the next two years are forecast to be marked with softer economic growth with slowing inflation. These trends will pave the way for a looser monetary policy.

Forget a rate cut or hike

For the foreseeable future, though, a rate cut is unlikely. As my preferred policy rate predictor, CME's FedWatch, shows below, the markets place an overwhelming probability on unchanged interest rates.

Interestingly, though, some 8.3% likelihood is accorded to a 25 basis points reduction in interest rates (See Chart 1 below). This is a swing back to the expectation of monetary easing after a small speculation that the Fed could actually raise interest rates following a healthy labor market report.

The Fed's latest dot plot rules out this possibility for now, however, considering that at best rates are seen remaining static (see Chart 2 below). There is some likelihood of more than one rate cut going forward, though. Based on the outlook for the year, it's tough to get behind even that right now, however. And definitely not in the next meeting, as the markets indicate.

Market non-response...

It's rare that the FOMC rate decision is a complete non-event for the stock markets. But yesterday was one of those days. All three key indices - the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY), NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX, QQQ), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) barely moved yesterday (see table below).

...but solid opportunities exist

From a sectoral perspective, cyclicals weakened the most, which was to be expected considering the unchanged rates' stance. And with the outlook for the economy less predictable than at most times, cyclical do need to be approached with some care.

While there are consumer stocks that still offer great value, it's hard to miss the current weakness in the healthcare sector. As a classic defensive at a time of uncertainty, which is probably slightly undervalued in the current environment, a closer look at it is merited.

Some of my recent coverage includes weight management treatment leaders Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY). While the segment is facing a downward pressure on price, which complicates an accurate determination of their financial potential, the fact that they are both trading at market multiples below their five-year averages isn't something to be ignored.

In conclusion

The latest FOMC statement reflects the complex nature of the current economic environment. While there isn't reason for the Fed to turn hawkish, it's not a time to ease interest rates either.

The updated economic projections also reflect on this complexity. While GDP growth is expected to slow down to below trend levels, there's little change seen in the unemployment rate. Also, while the inflation rate is expected to be higher in 2025 now than earlier estimated, by 2027, it will tend towards the Fed's target levels.

It's little wonder that the Fed is expected to keep rates unchanged next month as well. And going by the latest forecasts, it's possible for the rest of the year too. Next year, though, more monetary easing is possible.

The stock markets didn't respond at all to the FOMC statement, but the overall macroeconomic picture painted by the Fed indicates that defensives like healthcare, which have seen a recent pullback, can be attractive now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rare S&P 500 Stasis Reflects Key Investing Tenet
data.description:
By Breakingviews

The brain-trust behind the benchmark S&P 500 Index (SPX) made a bold move: it did nothing. For the first time in more than three years, no new companies were added to the tracker aimed at representing the overall market’s performance. It’s a reminder of an uncomfortable truth about investing: the best move is often just sitting tight.

The panel’s calculated restraint stems from a selection process that is both quantitatively rigorous and strategically discretionary. Analysts and economists from S&P Dow Jones Indices, a joint venture between financial data giant S&P Global and exchange operator CME, evaluate potential index candidates against exacting criteria, which include at least a $20.5 billion market value and 50% public float. This time around, they passed on 27 eligible entrants, including money manager Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), online car dealership Carvana (CVNA) and specialty insurer Markel Group (MKL), among others.

For investment shops that feast on readjusted stock baskets, the status quo lands at an awkward time. Once-reliable arbitrage opportunities have delivered increasingly marginal returns since 1995, according to S&P Dow Jones Indices. Index rebalancing teams at Millennium Management, the $61 billion hedge fund manager, lost $900 million this year through March simply from market convulsions, Bloomberg reports.

The chart displays median excess returns versus the S&P 500 index for additions and deletions from the announcement date to the effective date.

Additions and subtractions to the S&P 500 go broadly unnoticed by most investors. About 90% of such changes occur between scheduled quarterly updates, with an average of 25 companies entering and exiting annually. Most aren’t even committee decisions, but rather corporate inevitability. Roughly 70% of departures result from events such as mergers and acquisitions.

This month’s standstill provides everyday investors with a rare respite from Wall Street’s byzantine machinery. Institutional rebalancing costs index followers roughly $16 billion annually by creating exploitable price patterns, according to a study published in March. When indices are due to adjust, hedge funds try to capitalize on predictable trades that big funds must execute, parasitically forcing them to pay inflated prices.

Deliberate inertia by S&P 500 Index designers mirrors an important hallmark of longer-term investing. Today’s volatile mix of geopolitical and policy unrest makes the impulse to act nearly irresistible, and nearly always wrong. The only thing more dependable than owning a broadly diversified gauge of large stocks is one with the discipline to leave well enough alone.

Context News

  • S&P Dow Jones Indices said on June 6 that it had made no changes to S&P 500 Index constituents, the first unchanged quarterly rebalancing since March 2022.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Henderson Land: Turning Bullish On New Lease And Favorable Rate Environment (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My rating for Henderson Land Development Company (OTCPK:HLDCY) (OTCPK:HLDVF) (0012.HK) or "HLDC" is lifted to Buy.

I was previously Neutral on HLDC in the March 21, 2025 write-up, considering the group's poor 2H24 financials and its shares' attractive pricing.

HLDCY is now deserving of a Buy in my opinion. The stock's risk-reward has become more favorable, taking into account the latest property deal and recent interest rate movements.

Fresh Lease Has Positive Read-Throughs For Commercial Market

A week ago, on June 13, HLDVF revealed that it rented out roughly one-third ("or 70% of the initial phase of development") of the total office area for the NCH ("New Central Harbourfront") project.

Overview Of "New Central Harbourfront"

The major takeaway is the HK ("Hong Kong") office sector is much healthier than feared, which would be good for HLDC.

It's natural to worry that fewer enterprises will take up new workspace in this city with the ongoing Sino-American tariff war. But that assertion isn't true.

The tenant is financial firm "Jane Street" or "JS". It's encouraging to know U.S. companies are still keen on having a Hong Kong base to exploit China/Asia growth opportunities. The size of JS's premises there is estimated to increase by over +120% with the NCH lease.

The HKD 137 psfpm ("per square feet per month") headline rental for the JS-NCH deal was more than 50 percent higher than the average ("HK$90") for the same area ("Grade A Central HK offices") in early-2025. The effective rent might be a tad lower, as it will be usual practice for the landlord to offer some concessions ("e.g. rent-free initial periods"). But the wide gap ("between headline and industry average numbers") still shows that businesses are willing to pay a premium for brand-new, well-located ("Central, HK") workplaces.

It's also telling that JS entered into the leasing agreement about 30 months ("January 1, 2028 rental agreement start date") before it can move into its new office. If the HK commercial property industry was in bad shape, this American entity wouldn't have rushed to lock-in the space at NCH that early in advance.

HLDCY is a real estate company with a balance between "Property Development" ("PD") and "Property Investment" ("PI") activities. It generated earnings of HK$6.5 billion and HKD 5.6B from its PI and PD segments, respectively last year. Offices in turn represented 38 percent ("Gross Floor Area terms") of HLDVF's Hong Kong investment properties as per its annual report disclosures.

The future performance of HLDC's office sub-segment is likely to surprise investors in a positive manner. The NCH-JS transaction implies that demand for business premises in HK remains strong.

Lower HKD Reference Rate Is A Tailwind

Easier credit conditions will be beneficial for HLDCY.

The 1M and 3M "Hong Kong Interbank Offered Rate" ("HIBOR") or "Hong Kong Dollar Interest Settlement Rate" ("HDISR") are down -404 and -284 bps ("basis points"), respectively year-to-date ("up to June 19, 2025"). Considering the HKD-USD peg and the outlook for Fed rate cuts, it is likely this ("downward") trend will be sustained.

HK residential mortgages' pricing is usually pegged to the "one-month" HDISR; while corporates often borrow at rates referencing the "three-month" figure.

HLDVF's Residential Developments Scheduled For Launch This Year

One will likely be interested to buy a new unit at one of HLDC's newest property projects ("presented above") with the ("1M") benchmark rate decline.

Your monthly interest installments would be more manageable thanks to cheaper property loans. Assuming one intends to rent out his or her property in the future, the net carry ("rental yield exceeding decreased mortgage rate") will be another "pull factor" to make home purchases in the near-term.

The prospects for HLDVF's PD business have gotten better as explained above.

Separately, HLDC's financial burden should become lighter.

HLDCY took part in HSBC Holdings' (HSBC) late-March investor event. The bank published a report ("not publicly available") titled "Global Investment Summit Takeaways" in end-Mar. According to this research note, HLDVF stressed at the conference that it will "focus on debt reduction to improve its financial flexibility."

It's reassuring to know that HLDC has deleveraging as a priority. Its "adjusted net debt-to-equity" was 42% as mentioned in my March-25 article.

Close to fourth-fifths ("including related-party loans, source: FY24 annual report") of HLDC's borrowings are on variable-rate terms. This is likely linked to the 3M-HDISR which is dropping.

My estimate is that HLDVF's bottom-line can go up around +7% for every 1ppt ("percentage point") decrease in floating-rate debt cost.

Bear-Case Scenarios You Would Consider

A worldwide recession triggered by geopolitical conflicts or tariffs will be a huge headwind for HLDCY's cyclical property business.

A sharp rebound in the HK dollar reference rate could translate into weaker home sales and higher interest costs for the group.

The Final Word

I'm a bull on HLDVF, after assessing rate changes and real estate transactions of late.

HLDCY is undervalued with the stock trading at just two-fifths ("source: S&P Capital IQ") of NTA ("Net Tangible Assets").

The upside for HLDC is +30% in the scenario its P/NTA expands to the 10-year average of 0.52x.

I think that mean reversion is a fairly conservative valuation assumption. Interest rates are in the company's favor, and the latest lease suggests that HK's offices are still sought-after.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank Of England Set To Cut Rates In August After June Pause
data.description:
By Francesco Pesole & James Smith

Macro background more favourable for cuts

The Bank of England has kept rates on hold at 4.25% and, more importantly, has offered little indication that the recent batch of worrisome labour market data warrants a faster pace of easing. Taken at face value, the latest payrolls data showed the fastest monthly fall in employment outside of the pandemic since records for the figures began in 2014 – and these have now fallen in 9 out of the past 10 months. They do, however, have a tendency of being revised up, and for now, officials aren’t sounding the alarm bells on a more serious uptick in unemployment. Policymakers are not helped by the fact that several of the major data releases – including the unemployment rate, GDP and inflation – have all recently been subject to data quality issues or excessive volatility. The consensus at the Bank is, understandably, to wait for more information.

But we think the backdrop is becoming more favourable. Services inflation is likely to come under further downward pressure over the coming months, while wage growth is undershooting the Bank’s May forecasts. Given how far the jobs market has cooled (and continues to), we suspect wage growth will come materially lower by the end of this year.

Two more cuts in 2025

Does that mean faster rate cuts? For now, we think it’s unlikely. Yes, three out of nine officials voted for an immediate rate cut this time. Investors may well take that as a hint that the tide is turning on the committee. But past experience has shown that the vote split contains few useful signals. December’s meeting saw a similar 6-3 vote, yet heralded little change in the Bank’s overall stance.

The hawks, meanwhile, will also have a beady eye on oil prices. Though the rise so far won't make much difference to the inflation outlook – and officials commented that it had little bearing on this latest decision – we know some at the Bank are wary of a repeat of 2022, where a rise in energy prices turned into a much wider and more persistent services-driven inflation episode.

The wider circumstances today look very different, though. For one thing, the labour market is much looser today than it was back then, offering less scope for workers to recoup disposable income lost to higher oil prices. That said, a much more serious spike in oil prices is the most obvious hawkish risk for the UK rate outlook right now. That aside, our base case is that the BoE cuts rates in August and November, and twice more in 2026.

EUR/GBP balance of risks still tilted to the upside

The pound’s reaction to the BoE announcement was very muted, as the vote split seemed to merely endorse markets' well-established expectations for a cut in August.

We retain a short-term target in EUR/GBP at 0.860. Geopolitical risks and a potential return of trade-induced market volatility in July argue that the balance of risks remains tilted to the upside for the pair despite the recent rally.

There is also a non-negligible possibility that another batch of soft UK data can prompt markets to speculate on an acceleration in BoE rate cuts. When adding the additional risks to UK growth, the jobs market and the prospect of fiscal tightening, we think the pound can underperform most G10 currencies in the second half of 2025, with EUR/GBP breaking above 0.870.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Has CrowdStrike Avoided The Fallout From The Great Outage?
data.description:
Almost a year ago, CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) experienced a worldwide outage, causing Windows machines to not boot up with "blue screens of death." It brought down critical infrastructure and caused widespread panic among the public, as computers used to aid logistics, banks, hospitals, airlines, and other essential services came to a halt. After the post-mortem and aftermath, investors and Wall Street peeked through their fingers and saw the company was still financially sound and running. They came out not long after and said, "All is clear!"

But not so fast.

In September, I said the jury was still very much out. Enterprise software doesn't work that quickly. Moreover, the upside of a subscription-based business model is the predictability of revenue and customer churn. The downside is when an adverse event hits the company's business, there are no immediate signs of fallout, and a false sense of security begins to shroud the numbers. As earnings reports approach the anniversary of the disastrous event (from a software perspective), we are now going to see the results - good, bad, or indifferent.

The recent mediocre earnings report had me sensing something was afoot. Let's find out what is ailing CrowdStrike.

Cybersecurity is one of the fastest-growing sectors in technology, trailing only those directly related to AI. Out of the six major cybersecurity companies, only one missed earnings this quarter. That one was CrowdStrike. The same went for guidance; there was only one, the same one.

Is the earnings shortfall a correlation as we approach the anniversary of the event, or is there a larger canary in the coal mine event for cybersecurity overall? Maybe neither, but I'll consider whether it's due to the bug event or if this is merely a temporary financial setback on the path to further growth.

The FQ1 '26 earnings report showed the company generated $1.1B in revenue, which was $10M lower than consensus. Additionally, guidance for FQ2 was $1.148B, which was below the $1.16B estimate. Two out of two misses of consensus, where those estimates were already on a downtrend since the fall of last year, and remained wholly flat from then on, is not a good start.

Alright, so why the miss?

There are ongoing headwinds to the CCP (customer consolidation program) from the IT outage in July, which concluded a quarter ago (FQ4 '25). The impact of this program endures because, although the program's sign-up deadline has passed, its financial effects are now being amortized.

As a result of these CCP-related programs, we expect to see a temporary near-term separation between ARR and subscription revenue recognition, which was reflected in our revenue guidance. This amounted to approximately $11 million in Q1. We expect the impact to be in the range of $10 million to $15 million in each remaining quarter of this fiscal year, subsiding in Q4.

- Burt W. Podbere, CFO, CrowdStrike's FQ1 '26 Earnings Call

$11M in Q1 is within the margin for missing the consensus for Q1. With it, the company would have beaten by $1M. Likewise, guidance for Q2 missed by $12M, while the impact of this CCP headwind is $12.5M at the midpoint.

Even with this accounted for, it only lands at the headline level - a beat and raise would be what you would have read. But it would have been by the skin of its teeth, not the large beats and raises peers have been seeing. For example, Zscaler (ZS) beat by nearly $11M, with almost half the revenue baseline (resulting in a larger percentage beat).

So, why the relative underperformance compared to peers?

Management has been encouraging customers to opt for its Falcon Flex program, where the company and customers negotiate an overarching contract with access to various products and modules, along with a defined set of usage (licenses). It allows flexibility in using a library of products up to the negotiated consumption amount. It's more of a broad 'token-based' system I've used with other companies in my work, where it becomes a company-based currency to use across many of its products instead of negotiating contracts per product for a set number of licenses. This avoids renegotiating more licenses for that specific product or starting the sales cycle from the beginning to use another product the company has, also with a locked-in number of licenses. This program offers flexibility in choosing where to apply licenses across software products.

It's not a new concept, but it's novel in the cybersecurity industry.

So why does this program matter?

While this program isn't exactly like transitioning from perpetual licensing to subscription-based licensing, it is a degree lower in terms of transitioning the subscription business model. The way management has it structured, a larger purchase has a larger discount. But the idea is the customer no longer has any barriers to the entire ecosystem of products. It can add and use new products up to the licensing limit negotiated. This builds out the ecosystem and roots the customer into the company's products. In terms of financials, while it provides another level of retention, it isn't as lucrative upfront, just like subscription-based licensing was from perpetual. This creates a minor headwind as customers transition to it and new customers go directly to it.

[We] added $774 million of total Falcon Flex account value, bringing the total deal value of accounts that have adopted Falcon Flex to $3.2 billion, growing 31% sequentially and more than 6x year-over-year.

- George R. Kurtz, CEO, CrowdStrike's FQ1 '26 Earnings Call

The most significant sales headwind we've heard from a myriad of cybersecurity companies over the past few years has been an elongated sales cycle. CrowdStrike is taking that head-on and changing the way the sales cycle looks, reducing cycle time to close deals. But since the incentive is to purchase more for larger discounts, it results in what would otherwise be a larger contract value in a short time period and spreads it out a lower price over a longer period, thus recognizing less revenue within each quarter. The advantage to the company is the easier ramp to a larger contract, as the friction is less by renegotiating only the license amount. Overall, this is likely the cause of some headwinds in the revenue department.

In other words, the company is undergoing a lower-degree subscription model transition, and just like the subscription transitions we've seen across software companies over the last several years, there's a headwind upfront that leads to growth acceleration on the back end. With Flex, it could prove to be powerful, as some customers with 30+ month contracts have come back within five months to renegotiate more licensing.

This brings me to the final point: what about the outage and customer retention?

While we haven't fully lapped the incident, as Q1 ended in April and Q2 ends in July, the month the event occurred last year, it doesn't appear many customers have jumped ship, or at least not yet. The CEO claims it sported a 97% retention rate in Q1. This is one of the highest I've seen in the industry. But again, the problem is we haven't allowed the time it takes for customers to rip and replace vendors and their products to happen. If the Q3 guide is decent next quarter, then I'll say all is clear on that front.

Thus far, I can account for other circumstances contributing to the lack of robust earnings.

So, has CrowdStrike made it out unscathed? It's looking better and better with each passing quarter, but until it's lapped, it's hard to say. Keep in mind the timeline I outlined in the September article when it comes to enterprise sales cycles.

That being said, with the company's fundamentals remaining somewhat uncertain, its chart reflects that sentiment. With a nice recovery after the July incident last year into all-time highs, the party may be in the late hours already.

Being we're in circle 5, the chart is seemingly in the latter half of its third wave. The problem is this is an aggressive count of circle 5, and I don't have a lot of confidence in it at this point, but it's the one that makes the most sense so far.

While the stock has recovered nicely from the July outage and pushed into new highs, the rally is approaching its final targets. These upper targets are between $610 and $620 and are in play as long as $426 holds, with $394 as final support. If both support levels aren't held, then the upside targets are void. This is the later stages of CrowdStrike's all-time high rally. Caution is warranted here as targets are being met on varying wave degrees.

So while we may get the all clear in another quarter in terms of customer retention, the chart is looking tired. The risk up here is more uncertain than it ever has been. And with headwinds from the remnants of the outage and transition to a new subscription model, the growth isn't there for the next few quarters to sustain the returns outside of what I outlined on the chart.

data.title: TSLZ: A Tactical Tool Waiting In The Wings
data.description:
In October 2023, T-Rex 2X Inverse Tesla Daily Target ETF (BATS:TSLZ) was the first single-stock 2X inverse leveraged ETF to debut on U.S. bourses. That gives us about a year and nine months of performance data, but rather than looking at long-term returns right away, it is more prudent to explore how the daily reset on this ETF works, and how you can possibly use it as a vehicle to take advantage of declines in the underlying asset, which is Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) stock.

I covered TSLZ back in March of this year. At the time, I recommended it as a downside capture tool for TSLA shareholders. I still do, but I think now is a far more appropriate time to learn how to use this quasi-shorting tool on TSLA. The bond between Trump and Musk is broken, and in my view, it is not beneath Trump to undertake a personal vendetta against his erstwhile 'best bud'. Case in point: The White House has now directed NASA and the Department of Defense to "review" SpaceX contracts, with a view to possibly limiting its role in the Golden Dome.

That aside, Tesla has been struggling on several fronts lately: sales slumps across multiple markets, general animosity toward the actions taken during his short-lived bureaucratic stint, and ongoing challenges in the China supply chain equation have all contributed to poor Q1 performance for the world's most iconic EV brand.

Likelihood of a Decline in TSLA Stock

Honestly speaking, my Sell rating on TSLA did not hold up too well, but I think that was more a case of misplaced optimism on the part of bullishly entrenched shareholders. The stock was at $270 or so when I last covered TSLZ, surging to over $360 in a matter of weeks. Of late, it has pulled back to around $320 to $325 and seems to be holding steady, relatively speaking. There is no doubt that it has always been a volatile stock, so that is not about to change, but I think investors are growing increasingly impatient for Magician Musk to pull the next rabbit out of his hat.

This is where I turn contrarian, as I point out the problem, which is that the current scenario makes TSLZ a poor tool to capture TSLA downside, at least for the time being. Regardless, investors should be prepared for the worst. We shall see why, and we shall also see how the stock's inherent volatility can be used to generate handsome returns whenever TSLA hits a downswing. For now, it seems unlikely that TSLA will continue to decline. If at all, it will purely be based on fundamentals. In itself, that is an oddity for a company that has long been valued based on promises made by the CEO.

What is Likely to Happen with TSLA?

Contrary to most investors' expectations for TSLA to have a further downside, I do not think that will happen in the short term. First, we shall look at what the experts think.

Seeking Alpha's Quant rating for TSLA has been a Hold for the longest time. Since the pandemic struck, there has only been a very brief time when Quant carried a Strong Buy rating.

Much of this had to do with the Revisions grade, where Street analysts have swung widely between A+ and F, depending on how earnings trended at those times. The other factor grade that has been responsible for the overall Hold over the last five years has been Growth.

Together, these two factor grades have kept Quant from rating Tesla a Buy or a Strong Buy. Right now, it is TSLA's valuation and downward revisions by analysts that are maintaining the Hold rating. Valuation has always been a challenge with this stock, since it typically factors in new products or features that have yet to hit the market or are based on Musk's tweeted/x-ed commitments. Indeed, it has rarely moved above an F since Quant started covering it in August 2019; you can see this if you choose the 3-year Quant rating option on this page.

Wall Street ratings, as captured on Seeking Alpha, are a similar story. Analysts on the Street have never rated it higher than a Buy, and even that was only when TSLA traded at under $290 between April and August 2023.

Even Seeking Alpha analysts haven't been very optimistic about the stock. As a matter of fact, there were several periods when TSLA was rated a Sell.

The broad theme seems to be a Hold on the stock, and for various reasons. I have rated Tesla a Sell in the past, but there is something about the stock that has defied expert opinion over the past five years, during which period it has gained over 400%. Said differently, the downswings appear to be the effect of news, while upswings seem to be the broad bullish view of Tesla's mostly-next-generation products and features like full self-driving, robots, robotaxis, and the like - with the end result being the stock keeps moving upward.

If that changes, and that is something that can easily happen overnight, then TSLZ would be the perfect tool to take advantage of it.

How to Play TSLZ on TSLA Downswings

Since TSLZ is leveraged to capture twice the downside of TSLA, it is logical to assume that investors should only use it as a tactical tool. This is not intended to be a buy and hold security; on the contrary because of the daily reset, it is not prudent to hold it for longer than a few days, at the most.

The leverage itself is achieved through the use of total return swaps, and is structured in a way that allows the fund to make twice the amount that TSLA loses on any given trading day.

Buying these ETF shares is equal to participating in this leveraged play. Theoretically, if TSLA drops by an X amount, TSLZ will increase in value by 2X. On every trading day when TSLA closes lower than it opens is a gain day for the ETF's shares. This is exactly why it is a risky play because that leverage works in the opposite direction when TSLA gains on a given trading day.

The big risk here is that the ETF resets daily, and this is an important aspect of leveraged ETFs one should fully understand and appreciate. Suffice it to know that holding the fund for prolonged periods could potentially lead to something known as slippage or decay, and that is the direct result of the leverage applied to the ETF's performance. It works the same way for unleveraged products as well, but the effect is amplified 2X with this particular class of ETFs.

So, how does one know when to invest in TSLZ? Although the mechanics of the total return swaps are complex, one can better understand their results by studying the price momentum of the underlying TSLA asset. Let us look at an example of how TSLZ moves against a declining TSLA.

Between TSLA's peak in December 2024 and the trough in March 2025, it lost a little more than 50 percent of its market capitalization. Look at how much TSLZ gained in that time, and this despite the decay we talked about earlier. The point being that in a steady downtrend (for TSLA), TSLZ has the potential to give investors a lot more than the 2X inverse returns that the ETF aims to achieve.

I know this goes against what I said earlier about not holding this instrument for more than a day or so, and I maintain that because nobody can predict a prolonged downturn; more importantly, nobody can predict when it might reverse and wipe out all or a significant portion of their gains until then.

What one can do is use momentum indicators such as RSI to determine the best entry and exit points for TSLZ, the tactic being to buy when TSLA is in overbought territory and sell before it hits oversold.

This relatively simple strategy can allow you to repeatedly gain significant returns in the long run.

Is it Worth the Risk?

That is the question to ask yourself because I have no idea what your risk tolerance level is. Just keep in mind that the losses made on a TSLZ trade are very hard to reverse - again, due to the daily reset.

My opinion is that TSLA shareholders would do well to use this as a sort of hedge to offset declines that last more than a day, and that is something that momentum indicators like RSI should help you do.

As for my recommendation, a Hold is the only appropriate one as far as I am concerned. Although the risk is elevated, so is the reward.

data.title: PayPal: Stablecoin Will Power Growth
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Shares of PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) have had a rough first half of 2025. Year-to-date, shares of the financial technology company are down nearly 17%. With the rise of stablecoins the reasons for PayPal’s underperformance has been due to fears that Apple Pay and Shopify are encroaching on PayPal’s checkout share along with stablecoins encroaching on their payment processing fees.

With this, however, I really believe that these concerns are overstated. PayPal has entrenched merchant relationships and high-conversion funnels.

I want to focus on management’s “win checkout” push for 2025 - particularly the Fastlane one-click guest checkout launched in early 2024, which I wrote about extensively during my last piece of coverage on PayPal. I view this as a direct effort to streamline purchases and defend PayPal’s core franchise.

I believe that PayPal’s vast network of over 400 million accounts between both consumers and merchants creates durable scale advantages that are hard for new entrants like Apple Pay and Shopify to replicate. In my opinion, there is promising optionality in PayPal’s growth stories - most notably crypto - highlighted by the 2023 rollout of the PYUSD stablecoin, which could help lower transaction costs and open new rails for payments. With legislation moving through Washington DC that promises regulations for Stablecoins, this could be a huge catalyst. Paypal should benefit.

With this, I continue to believe shares are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

Since I published my last coverage on PayPal, shares of the company dipped by as much as 21%, but they have since rebounded, and they’re down just about 2% since late February when I last published coverage on PayPal. I think most of this recent selloff has been driven by Apple Pay and Shopify fears, echoing mid-2022 worries that ultimately proved unfounded when Apple integrated PayPal as a checkout option.

I’ve seen anecdotal bear arguments gain some traction, but I think these are isolated and are definitely not reflective of PayPal’s broad merchant base.

Something that keeps me bullish on PayPal heading into the rest of the year is CEO Alex Chriss relentless focus on winning back market share as his top priority. Fastlane is a big part of this story.

With this, early metrics continue to reassure me. As noted on the Q4 call:

75% of Fastlane consumers are new or dormant PayPal users. This means that Fastlane not only improves conversion for our merchants, but also introduces more shoppers to PayPal and enables us to reengage in active users.

With the growth of their stablecoin + stronger regulatory backing in DC for stablecoin, I think this makes now a perfect time to revisit PayPal. I believe the recent pullback is just short-term noise. I think the bull case is accelerating.

Stablecoin Deep Dive

To understand why Stablecoins are a big deal for PayPal, I want to focus on PayPal USD and why this is such a big development for PayPal. PayPal USD (or PYUSD) was launched in August 2023 as the first US-backed stablecoin from a major fintech company. The stablecoin was issued via Paxos Trust, is fully collateralized by dollar deposits and U.S. treasuries, and is pegged 1:1 to the U.S. dollar.

With growing adoption (and a more favorable environment in DC) we’ve seen a trend begin to develop where stablecoins are being brought into mainstream payments. PYUSD is being integrated across both PayPal and Venmo for P2P transfers, Xoom remittances, and third-party wallet payments. The goal here is to leverage blockchain rails to undercut card network fees and settlement times.

I think PayPal’s 400 million+ user base and merchant integrations give PYUSD a huge distribution edge in both commerce use cases and remittances - both of which are situations where crypto-native stablecoins have struggled to gain traction.

To add to this, in April 2025, PayPal partnered with Coinbase to waive fees on PYUSD buys and sells and enable one-click conversion to USD. In the future, I expect the roadmap to enable merchants to settle directly in PYUSD. This could be huge and help eliminate fees with cross-border transactions.

PayPal plans to roll out a 3.7% APY yield on PYUSD balances in PayPal and Venmo wallets starting in the summer. What this means is that it will make holding PYUSD materially more attractive than cash or rival stablecoins.

I view PYUSD as a catalyst for seriously accelerating the amount of cash PayPal holds on its balance sheet that it can generate interest on. In a way, PayPal becomes closer to a bank, which could have huge implications on net income margins.

The potential for this market could be huge, with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent estimating that the TAM of stablecoins could exceed $2 Trillion in just a few years.

If Paypal grabbed 20% market share with its stablecoin ($400 billion) and then make a 1% net income margin spread on it ($4 billion) this could go a long way to increase their bottom line.

Valuation

Currently, PayPal has a forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 13.88. This is a 33.85% premium to fintech/financial peers, with the sector median currently sitting at 10.37. However, this 13.88 P/E ratio by PayPal is more than 51% below the company’s five-year average of 28.66. Seeking Alpha Quant gives PayPal a grade of a D+ on this metric.

PayPal’s forward price-to-book ratio of 3.22 is almost 196% higher than the sector median of 1.09. Meanwhile, similar to their forward price-to-earnings ratio, this mark is about 50% below the five-year average for PayPal. The company earns a grade of a D- on this metric. In my opinion, these two metrics reflect PayPal’s profitability. The market still values the company at a higher multiple than a traditional bank (more tech savvy) but not as high as other fintech leaders.

Interestingly, PayPal’s forward projected operating cash flow growth rate of 20.12% is more than double the sector median of 10.04%. This is given a grade of an B+ by Seeking Alpha Quant.

In essence, the company trades at a 33.85% premium to the sector median on a forward P/E ratio yet has forward cashflow growth that is projected to be more than double the sector median over the next 12 months (4 quarters).

In essence, I think the company’s forward P/E ratio should be at least a 60% premium to the sector median given this well above sector median growth.

If we saw their P/E premium expand to a 60% premium, this would represent 77.25% upside from here.

We get this upside by taking the projected premium (60% premium) and dividing it by the current premium (33.85%).

Frankly, I think even this 60% premium may turn out to be conservative. If we price in an additional $4 billion in net income from a 20% market share in the stablecoin market, I think this will make their cash flow growth even stronger than expected.

Risks

PayPal (despite their share price performance) should be a winner from the stablecoin revolution. That doesn’t mean they are risk free.

The biggest risk from the stablecoin revolution, in my opinion, is the intense fintech competition. I see Stripe’s global stablecoin push (their Bridge acquisition) and Apple/Shopify checkout integrations as credible threats to PayPal’s market share and fee-take rates.

Another risk I see is how ingrained some of the early movers are. It’s clear to me that USDC and USDT have cemented themselves as leaders. Switching would require clear benefits (their account yield of 3.7% APY may accomplish this). The other benefit is that PayPal is going down the regulatory-sanctioned route for stablecoins. This means that as USDC and USDT (run by Tether) are subject to more audits, we should see the benefits of building a stablecoin with regulations in mind as a key strategic edge.

There have been industry rumors that some stablecoins like Tether are not 1:1 backed by liquid assets. What we are about to find (thanks to the stablecoin act called the GENIUS Act) is that stablecoins are about to become a market where participants (stablecoin issuers) ask for regulatory permission.

Not regulatory forgiveness. Most of finance works this way. As stablecoins move this way as well, PayPal should benefit.

Bottom Line

While shares have slumped year to date I remain confident in PayPal. I think they’ve established themselves as a trusted brand with high-conversion checkout funnels and two-sided network effects. I think these things underpin what I think is its durable digital-payments leadership and path to serious growth in the stablecoin space.

I view PYUSD as a long-term growth engine. If mainstream stablecoin usage takes off within PayPal’s ecosystem, it could meaningfully lower processing costs and deepen engagement. I think most of the bearish concerns are coming from Apple/Shopify/Braintree headwinds, but I think all of these are manageable and are largely priced in. This sets a low bar for positive surprises in 2025-26. I think Stable coins are the biggest part of the bull thesis. Stablecoins have the opportunity to be a $4 billion lift to net income alone just on effective net interest income margins.

I think the valuation is supported by really durable cash flow, which makes for a scenario where investors effectively get optional upside from new initiatives “for free” on top of the stable core business. If management can successfully execute on checkout innovation and stablecoin adoption, I still consider PayPal to be a strong-buy with asymmetric upside over the next 12-24 months. The market appears to be irrational. This should be a gift to investors who are patient.
